summary: Broad front-page HN product scout surfaced 10 concrete items after history dedupe.

items.name: RubyLLM
items.source_story_title: RubyLLM: A Ruby framework for all major AI providers
items.summary: A Ruby framework for major AI providers with production-friendly ergonomics.
items.why_included: Commenters said they were using it in production and praised the design.
items.evidence: The thread was strongly positive overall, with multiple users calling RubyLLM elegant and easy to use.

items.name: Krea 2
items.summary: An open-weights 12B image model with RAW and Turbo checkpoints.
items.why_included: Commenters liked the technical report and the fine-tuning path.
items.evidence: The thread praised the open-weight release, the permissive licensing, and the RAW-to-Turbo LoRA workflow.

items.name: Gemini 3.5 Flash computer use
items.summary: Google’s computer-use feature for browser and UI automation.
items.why_included: Commenters saw practical value for QA, accessibility, and legacy workflows.
items.evidence: The thread argued about guardrails and UX, but several commenters still found computer use useful for real tasks.

items.name: Nub
items.source_story_title: Show HN: Nub – A Bun-like all-in-one toolkit for Node.js
items.summary: A Bun-like Node toolkit that adds transpilation and hooks without replacing Node.
items.why_included: The author’s stock-Node compatibility story drew real production questions.
items.evidence: The thread focused on preload hooks, performance, and whether the tool is safe and useful for backend production use.

items.name: GitHub pull request limits
items.source_story_title: Pull request limits are cutting down the noise
items.summary: GitHub’s maintainer controls for throttling PR spam.
items.why_included: Commenters treated it as a meaningful anti-spam move for maintainers.
items.evidence: The thread called the change a solid move and debated stronger reputation and bypass signals for contributors.

items.name: git-annex
items.source_story_title: I taught a bucket to speak Git
items.summary: A Git extension for storing data on remotes like S3.
items.why_included: Commenters pointed to it as a practical S3-backed Git option.
items.evidence: The thread highlighted git-annex for client-side encryption and large-file support when using object storage.

items.name: ZeroFS
items.source_story_title: I taught a bucket to speak Git
items.summary: An S3-backed filesystem layer that makes Git speak object storage.
items.why_included: Commenters said it may be faster than direct S3 remotes for big repos.
items.evidence: The thread described ZeroFS as a cleaner way to mount S3-backed storage and avoid Git-to-object-store gymnastics.

items.name: peerd
items.source_story_title: Show HN: peerd – AI agent harness that runs entirely in your browser
items.summary: A browser-extension agent harness that runs entirely inside the browser.
items.why_included: The browser-native sandbox and p2p direction drew real interest.
items.evidence: Commenters focused on the in-browser architecture, WebRTC contacts, and the promise of no external agent middlemen.

items.name: Bunny DNS
items.summary: Managed DNS from Bunny that is now free.
items.why_included: Commenters liked the move as a competitive EU alternative to Cloudflare.
items.evidence: The thread praised the BunnyNet team and repeatedly framed the free tier as a practical small-project option.

items.name: MonoLisa v3
items.source_story_title: Show HN: Monolisa v3 – a typeface for developers and creatives
items.summary: A developer typeface expanding into a proportional text family.
items.why_included: The design drew real discussion even amid price and licensing controversy.
items.evidence: Commenters praised the look and readability while arguing hard about cost, ligatures, and font policy.
